nativeRange.habitat: The Pea Puffer is native to the rivers, lakes, and backwaters of Kerala in southwestern India, where it lives close to the bottom in densely vegetated, slow-moving water.
nativeRange.biotope: Kerala freshwater — soft substrate with heavy aquatic vegetation; slow to moderate current

decorPreferences: Dense live planting (Java fern, Hornwort, Amazon sword, floating plants)
Multiple visual barriers and territory breaks
Pest snail colony (as supplemental food and enrichment)

diet.notes: Tends to refuse dried/prepared foods; generally requires live or frozen protein. Frozen bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and copepods are commonly accepted. Live or frozen blackworms are relished. Pest snails (ramshorn, bladder snails) are important for dental health — beak/teeth overgrowth has been reported without hard-shelled prey. Variety is important; single-food diets have been associated with nutritional decline.

goodWith: Fast-moving small tetras (ember tetra, chili rasbora) in larger tanks with dense cover
Otocinclus (reported to be largely ignored in many setups)
avoidWith: Long-finned or slow-moving fish (bettas, guppies, angelfish)
Large or fast fish that may stress the puffer
Snails intended as tank inhabitants (will be eaten)

summary: The Pea Puffer (Carinotetraodon travancoricus) is widely regarded as the world's smallest pufferfish, reaching no more than 3.5 cm as an adult. Native to Kerala, India, it is an exclusively freshwater species with a bold personality and a strict diet of live or frozen prey.
careNotes:
The Pea Puffer is assessed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List, primarily due to over-collection for the aquarium trade and ongoing habitat degradation. Captive breeding at commercial scale has not been established, so most specimens in the trade are wild-caught. Sourcing from reputable captive-bred breeders is encouraged when that option is available.

Despite belonging to the family Tetraodontidae — the same family as larger marine puffers — C. travancoricus is a true freshwater species and should NOT be kept in brackish or marine conditions.

Conspecific aggression: Males are territorial and will fight one another, often inflicting bite wounds. A ratio of one male to two or three females, combined with dense planting to break sightlines, is widely advised to reduce conflict. Injured fish should be isolated promptly.

Teeth/beak overgrowth: A condition sometimes called lockjaw has been documented in specimens fed exclusively soft foods. Regular access to hard-shelled prey — pest snails such as ramshorn or bladder snails, or frozen shellfish — helps wear down the beak naturally and is generally considered an important part of long-term care.

Toxicity note: Like other members of Tetraodontidae, this species likely carries tetrodotoxin (TTX), a naturally derived compound from its wild diet. In captivity on frozen and live foods, TTX accumulation is generally considered minimal, and the species is not commonly reported to pose a tank-wide toxin risk in the way some boxfishes can.

Grouping: A minimum group of three — typically one male to two or three females — is the commonly recommended starting configuration. Solo keeping is possible, but lone individuals tend to be more withdrawn.
breedingNotes: The Pea Puffer is an egg-scatterer that has been bred in captivity, though it requires some preparation. Males in breeding condition typically display vivid green and yellow coloration with dark patches on the belly. Eggs are deposited among fine-leaved plants or Java moss, and neither parent guards them. The eggs hatch in approximately 5 to 7 days. Fry are very small at hatching and have been reported to require infusoria or paramecia as a first food, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Because adults may eat both the eggs and newly hatched fry, removing adults after spawning or separating the eggs is generally advisable to improve survival rates.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or Java moss with no parental care following spawning. Fry require infusoria or paramecia initially, then baby brine shrimp nauplii as they develop. Separating adults after spawning or removing eggs to a dedicated container improves fry survival.
